text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Multidisciplinary Hepatobiliary Tumor Board Review / Treatment Assessment

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Medical Oncology Attending]
SERVICE: Medical Oncology / GI Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Outpatient Follow-Up, Post-Cycle 8 Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Gerald Holloway is a 58-year-old male with Stage IVA KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous hepatic metastases, presenting for follow-up after completion of cycle 8 of modified FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. This visit incorporates the results of a formal multidisciplinary hepatobiliary tumor board review of his most recent cross-sectional imaging.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Holloway was diagnosed approximately seven months ago with Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (pT3 pN2a M1a) with four synchronous hepatic metastases (segment VI, IVb, II, and VII), the largest measuring 3.1 cm at baseline. He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection approximately 5-6 weeks prior to initiating systemic therapy. Molecular profiling confirmed KRAS G12D mutation, MSS/pMMR status, BRAF wild-type, and NRAS wild-type, rendering him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy.

He was started on first-line modified FOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m2, 5-FU infusion 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg every 14 days, with the explicit intent of cytoreduction toward potential hepatic resection. Treatment was initiated with a curative-intent conversion chemotherapy framework.

At the cycle 6 restaging CT (approximately three months into treatment), he demonstrated a meaningful partial response in all four hepatic lesions -- the segment VI index lesion shrank from 3.1 cm to 1.9 cm, segment IVb from 2.2 cm to 1.4 cm, segment II from 1.4 cm to 0.8 cm, and segment VII from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm. Total target lesion burden declined approximately 39% from baseline. No new lesions were identified. His CEA trended down substantially from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis. This was a gratifying early response and the hepatobiliary surgery team was contacted for reassessment.

However, in the interval between cycles 6 and 8, Mr. Holloway developed worsening peripheral sensory neuropathy attributed to cumulative oxaliplatin exposure, progressing to grade 2 severity -- moderate persistent paresthesias in both hands and feet interfering with fine motor tasks such as buttoning shirts, as well as mild difficulty navigating uneven surfaces. As a result, the oxaliplatin dose was reduced by 25% to 65 mg/m2 beginning with cycle 7. His bevacizumab-associated hypertension also worsened during this period, requiring addition of amlodipine 5 mg daily to his existing lisinopril 20 mg, and is now better controlled.

He has now completed cycle 8. His most recent CEA is 52 ng/mL (down from 187 at baseline, though still elevated). He reports ongoing but tolerable fatigue. The neuropathy has not worsened since the oxaliplatin dose reduction. His blood pressure has been reasonably controlled on dual therapy at home.

Today's visit was scheduled specifically to discuss the results of the hepatobiliary tumor board review and to plan the trajectory of his therapy going forward.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Reports moderate fatigue, which he describes as 4/10 in severity; no significant weight loss since surgery (weight stable). No fevers or chills.
Neurological: Persistent bilateral hand and foot paresthesias and numbness, consistent with grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy. No new neurological symptoms. He notes the tingling in his hands has been somewhat better since the oxaliplatin dose reduction but the feet remain bothersome.
Gastrointestinal: Occasional mild nausea, managed at home with ondansetron as needed, no vomiting. Bowel function has normalized since colectomy -- 1-2 formed stools per day, no diarrhea or blood per rectum. No abdominal pain.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure has been running approximately 138-148/90-96 mmHg at home on lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 5 mg. No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea on exertion.
Muscuoloskeletal: Mild gait unsteadiness on uneven surfaces attributable to neuropathy; no falls.
Dermatologic: No skin changes, rash, or wound issues at the prior colectomy site.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Alert and oriented, well-nourished male appearing stated age. Mild fatigue evident but in good spirits. No acute distress.
Vital signs: Blood pressure 144/91 mmHg (right arm, sitting), heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 83 kg (stable from prior visit).
HEENT: No scleral icterus, no oral mucositis or thrush, no lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no jugular venous distension.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed laparoscopic port sites, no incisional hernia appreciated. No hepatosplenomegaly on palpation. No palpable masses. No ascites.
Extremities: Trace bilateral lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Reduced vibration sense in bilateral toes and dorsum of feet; reduced fine touch in bilateral fingertips. Gait slightly cautious but no frank ataxia. Romberg negative.
Skin: No rash, petechiae, or ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 4.2 x10^3/uL (ANC 2,480/uL -- within acceptable range)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, stable)
  - Platelets: 218 x10^3/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine: 0.92 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL, ALT: 44 U/L, AST: 39 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase: 91 U/L (all within or near normal limits)
  - Albumin: 3.7 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
  - CEA: 52 ng/mL (down from 187 ng/mL at baseline; note: slightly higher than mid-treatment nadir of the prior cycle assessment, possibly a plateau)
  - CA 19-9: 24 U/mL (down from 94 U/mL at diagnosis)

Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits. No evidence of bevacizumab-related coagulopathy.

---

IMAGING REVIEW:

The cycle 6 restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (most recent available, obtained approximately 3 weeks ago) was formally re-reviewed in the context of today's hepatobiliary multidisciplinary tumor board discussion. Key findings as pertinent to surgical planning:

Hepatic disease: All four liver lesions have responded to FOLFOX/bevacizumab. The dominant segment VI lesion is now approximately 1.9 cm, down from 3.1 cm at baseline. Segment IVb measures approximately 1.4 cm (from 2.2 cm), segment II approximately 0.8 cm (from 1.4 cm), and segment VII approximately 0.5 cm (from 0.9 cm). The distribution of disease involves bilateral lobes (segments II and IVb in the left lobe, VI and VII in the right lobe), which is a critical consideration for surgical planning. All four lesions have responded, but the bilateral involvement and the proximity of lesions to key vasculature limits the ability to perform a straightforward hepatectomy with adequate future liver remnant (FLR) without significant risk.

Extrahepatic disease: No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, retroperitoneal adenopathy, or other abdominal/pelvic metastases. Chest CT remains clear -- no pulmonary nodules or metastases identified.

Colectomy site: No evidence of anastomotic recurrence or local pelvic disease.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD SUMMARY:

This case was formally reviewed at today's hepatobiliary multidisciplinary tumor board in conjunction with hepatobiliary surgery, interventional radiology, and diagnostic radiology. Key discussion points:

1. Technical resectability: While all four lesions have responded meaningfully to systemic therapy and the absolute sizes are smaller, the hepatobiliary surgical team determined that the current configuration of disease -- bilateral bilobar distribution with lesions in segments II and IVb on the left and segments VI and VII on the right -- does not yet permit a safe, margin-negative (R0) hepatic resection while preserving an adequate future liver remnant. The estimated FLR after the resection currently required would be marginal, and the team was not comfortable proceeding with surgery at this juncture.

2. Trajectory: The response trajectory has been favorable, and continued systemic therapy over the next several cycles may allow for further consolidation and shrinkage -- particularly if the segment VI lesion (currently 1.9 cm) continues to decrease -- potentially rendering the resection more straightforward and the FLR more clearly adequate.

3. Recommendation from hepatobiliary surgery: Continue systemic therapy for an additional 4 cycles (cycles 9-12), then repeat cross-sectional imaging and formal hepatobiliary surgery re-review at that time. If the lesions continue to respond and FLR criteria are met, surgical planning will proceed.

4. Portal vein embolization (PVE): May need to be considered in the future to augment FLR if surgical planning warrants it, but this is premature at the current stage of response.

The patient was present for this discussion and the rationale was explained clearly. He understood and agreed with the recommendation to continue systemic therapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, BRAF wild-type, with bilobar hepatic metastases -- responding to first-line modified FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.
   - Disease is responding favorably to current regimen. While hepatic resection is not yet feasible due to bilobar distribution and FLR concerns, the overall response trajectory is encouraging.
   - Per hepatobiliary tumor board recommendation, will continue systemic therapy for 4 additional cycles (cycles 9-12), targeting a total of 12 cycles of FOLFOX-based therapy.
   - Repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be obtained after cycle 12 (approximately 2 months from now) for formal re-staging and hepatobiliary surgery reassessment.

2. Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, oxaliplatin-related.
   - Oxaliplatin dose reduction to 65 mg/m2 (from 85 mg/m2, 25% reduction) initiated at cycle 7 and maintained.
   - Will closely monitor neuropathy at each visit. If neuropathy worsens to grade 3 or becomes functionally disabling, oxaliplatin will be discontinued and the patient transitioned to maintenance 5-FU plus bevacizumab.
   - Neurontin (gabapentin) discussed as a potential symptomatic aid for the paresthesias; patient to trial 300 mg TID and report at next visit.

3. Bevacizumab-associated hypertension, now grade 1-2 on dual antihypertensive therapy.
   - Current regimen: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. Blood pressure today 144/91 mmHg -- acceptable for continued bevacizumab therapy.
   - Will monitor blood pressure at every infusion visit. If persistently above 160/100 mmHg, further medication adjustment will be undertaken. No bevacizumab dose modification needed at this time.
   - Patient instructed to continue home blood pressure monitoring and report readings above 160/100 mmHg.

4. Mild anemia of chronic disease.
   - Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, asymptomatic beyond baseline fatigue. Iron studies ordered to rule out superimposed iron deficiency (particularly relevant post-colectomy). No transfusion needed at this time.

5. Fatigue.
   - Multifactorial: cumulative chemotherapy effect, anemia, mild neuropathy-related deconditioning. Encouraged regular low-impact activity (walking). Referred to oncology rehabilitation/physical therapy to address gait and balance issues related to peripheral neuropathy and general deconditioning.

6. Clinical trial/future therapy discussion.
   - In the event of disease progression following completion of FOLFOX-based therapy, second-line options will be discussed at that time, including FOLFIRI-based regimens, anti-VEGFR approaches, and emerging KRAS G12D-directed investigational strategies. Patient is aware that his KRAS G12D mutation may make him eligible for certain clinical trials in the future.

Follow-up: Patient will return to clinic in approximately 2 weeks for cycle 9 day 1 labs and infusion. Next formal assessment visit with imaging will be scheduled approximately 6-8 weeks from now (post-cycle 12). Patient has contact information for the oncology nurse triage line and is instructed to call for any new symptoms, fever, significant blood pressure elevations, or worsening neuropathy.

ECOG Performance Status: 1
Cancer status: Present, responding to therapy.
Plan reviewed with patient and family; questions addressed.

[Electronically signed -- Medical Oncology Attending]
[Dictated and finalized -- Medical Oncology Fellow/Advanced Practice Provider]
